statusLine: Americas oil markets face elevated risks with WTI at $91.23/bbl after a 5.6% weekly decline, while critical supply route disruptions in Middle East shipping lanes threaten global crude flows.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets showed significant volatility in the week ending May 25, with WTI falling 5.56% to $91.23/bbl while maintaining its typical discount to Brent at $3.66. Despite the price decline, fundamental supply conditions remain relatively tight with US commercial crude stocks drawing down 9.9 million barrels to 374.2 million barrels, indicating robust domestic demand. US production held steady at 13.7 million bpd, maintaining America's position as the world's top producer.

The weekly crude inventory draw of nearly 10 million barrels represents one of the larger weekly declines this year, suggesting strong refinery runs and export demand are outpacing supply additions. Gasoline stocks also declined by 1.5 million barrels to 214.2 million barrels, while distillate inventories posted a modest 372,000 barrel build. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains at 81.6 million barrels, well below historical norms but stable.

Critical supply route risks are escalating globally, with multiple MARAD advisories flagging ongoing Houthi attacks in Red Sea shipping lanes and Iranian threats in the Strait of Hormuz. These chokepoints handle roughly 30% of global seaborne crude, and sustained disruptions could redirect tanker flows toward longer routes through the Panama Canal, potentially impacting Americas supply chains and pricing dynamics.

Retail fuel prices showed modest declines with gasoline averaging $4.62/gallon and diesel at $5.60/gallon, reflecting the crude price pullback but remaining elevated compared to historical norms. The relatively stable pricing environment masks underlying supply chain vulnerabilities as geopolitical tensions threaten key maritime corridors essential for global energy trade.
keyPoints: WTI crude fell 5.56% week-on-week to $91.23/bbl in the week ending May 25, widening discount to Brent to $3.66
US commercial crude inventories dropped 9.9 million barrels, signaling tight supply-demand balance despite price decline
Critical maritime disruptions in Red Sea and Strait of Hormuz pose escalating risks to global crude flows
US production steady at 13.7 million bpd, maintaining position as world's largest oil producer
Gasoline stocks declined 1.5 million barrels while distillate inventories posted modest build of 372,000 barrels
Retail fuel prices showing modest declines but remain elevated with gasoline at $4.62/gal and diesel at $5.60/gal
